does: This paper presents HeadVis, an interactive visualization system designed to expose and interpret the internal mechanisms of attention heads within language models. The tool enables researchers to inspect how individual attention heads allocate their attention weights across input tokens, revealing which parts of sequences different heads focus on during processing. By providing interactive exploration capabilities, HeadVis allows users to query attention patterns, identify recurring behavioral motifs across different inputs, and trace how information flows through attention mechanisms. The system supports both aggregate statistics over many examples and fine-grained analysis of specific attention head computations, helping practitioners understand what linguistic and structural phenomena individual heads capture.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of mechanistic interpretability research investigating transformer internals, extending earlier analyses of attention that typically relied on static visualizations or limited manual inspection. It connects to prior work on probing and analyzing what transformer components learn, including studies examining attention head specialization and the role of different heads in capturing syntax versus semantics. The paper responds to the challenge that raw attention weights are difficult to interpret at scale, building on visualization techniques from explainability research while adapting them specifically for the multi-head, multi-layer structure of modern language models. It complements concurrent efforts to decompose transformer behavior into interpretable components.
enables: HeadVis lays groundwork for deeper understanding of how language models process sequences, which could inform efforts to build more transparent and debuggable models. By making attention mechanisms more interpretable, the tool enables researchers to identify failure modes, understand model biases, and verify that models attend to linguistically meaningful patterns. This interpretability infrastructure could support mechanistic explanations of model capabilities and limitations, facilitate model editing and improvement, and help detect when models rely on spurious correlations. The interactive framework suggests a path toward interactive model debugging and hypothesis testing, potentially enabling more principled approaches to model design and safety validation.
